settings.title: Free Pruning Morning at the Yard This March
settings.excerpt: Bring a branch and a photo of your shrub and we will show you exactly where to make the cut.
settings.body:
Spring is the moment people reach for the secateurs and then freeze, unsure where to start. So we are opening the yard for a free pruning morning on the last Saturday of March.

Bring photos of the shrubs that puzzle you, and a sample branch if you can. We will demonstrate on our own stock plants and send you home knowing the difference between a renewal cut and a heading cut. Most of the worry is about cutting too much, and we will show you why a confident cut usually beats a timid one.

What to bring

  • Photos of the whole shrub, not just the bit that bothers you
  • A short sample branch in a bag if you can take one
  • Your own secateurs, if you would like us to check they are sharp

We will cover the common shrubs we see on the round: roses, hydrangeas, lavender, and the overgrown laurels that nobody knows what to do with. Each one wants a different timing and a different cut, and getting that wrong is why a lot of plants sulk and refuse to flower.

There will be tea, and a table of bare-root hedging at cost price for anyone who wants to plant before the season closes. Bare-root is the cheapest way to put in a hedge, but the window shuts once the plants break into leaf, so March is close to the last call.

No booking needed. Just turn up between nine and noon. If it is pouring, we will run it under the open shed, so do not let the forecast put you off.
